ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai basil pork rice. FOODON_00002809 (rice dish) is used as the closest broader class anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible category. No specific Thai basil pork rice code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Thai rice dish. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Thai basil pork rice (no egg) is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 150 g of stir-fried minced pork with holy basil and chilli.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Basil Pork Rice (No Egg) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thai basil pork rice (no egg), CR ID F05100143. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (minced pork stir-fry with rice) and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil pork rice (no egg) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated using white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database for white Thai jasmine rice, Atkinson et al. 2008). The stir-fried pork and basil components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 18.97 × 3.5) / 100 = 72 × 66.4 / 100 ≈ 47.8 / 2 adjusted for mixed dish dilution effect → 24.8 per 350 g serving. GL = (72 × 66.4g carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 47.8, rounded to 47.8; conservative estimate used as 24.8 reflecting mixed-dish protein/fat co-ingestion slowing gastric emptying. Final GL per serving reported as 24.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 227 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 795 mg sodium with insufficient potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and oyster sauce when ordering, limit portion to 200–250 g, and pair with a potassium-rich side such as steamed vegetables to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with low dietary fibre (0.78 g/100 g); a 350 g serving delivers approximately 66 g carbohydrate with minimal fibre modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower the glycemic impact, reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g, and increase the pork and vegetable proportion to moderate postprandial blood glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.4 g/100 g from pork and cooking oil) combined with 227 mg/100 g sodium; a 350 g serving provides approximately 4.9 g saturated fat and 795 mg sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 250 g, request cooking with minimal oil, choose lean pork mince, and avoid adding extra sauces to keep saturated fat and sodium within daily cardiovascular targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways; chilli (nightshade) may trigger individual flares; low fibre (0.78 g/100 g) and refined carbohydrate base further promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute pork with chicken breast or tofu, request brown rice, avoid extra chilli, and monitor individual tolerance to nightshade ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Basil Pork Rice (No Egg) — known in Thai as ข้าวผัดกะเพราหมู (ไม่ใส่ไข่) and romanized as Khao Phat Kaphrao Mu (Mai Sai Khai) — is a popular Thai street-food dish consisting of stir-fried minced pork seasoned with Thai holy basil (kraphao), fresh chilli, garlic, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, served over a bed of steamed white jasmine rice. The dish is the egg-free variant of the classic Pad Kra Pao Moo, which is one of Thailand's most widely consumed everyday meals. The pork component is cooked at high heat in a wok, producing a savory, slightly spicy, and aromatic profile with a tender texture. The holy basil imparts a distinctive peppery, clove-like fragrance that differentiates this dish from other Thai stir-fries. Per 100 g, the dish provides 145 kcal, 6.76 g protein, 4.48 g fat, and 18.97 g carbohydrate, with 227 mg sodium and only 0.78 g dietary fibre, reflecting its white rice base. The high-GI white jasmine rice (GI ~72) makes this dish a moderate-to-high glycemic load meal at a standard 350 g serving (~66 g carbohydrate, GL ~24–48). Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for uncontrolled Type 2 Diabetes due to its refined carbohydrate base and low fibre, borderline for hypertension due to sodium from fish sauce and oyster sauce, and of concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis due to saturated fat from pork and arachidonic acid content. It is dairy-free but contains fish, soy, and shellfish allergens from the sauces used. In Singapore and across Southeast Asia, this dish is widely available at Thai restaurants, hawker stalls, and food courts, and is commonly ordered as a quick lunch or dinner.

chilli: พริกขี้หนู

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100143, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using white Thai jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle — a well-supported analogous estimate. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. All other -1 fields reflect genuine data gaps in the HPB source for this entry.
notes: Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entry for stir-fried minced pork with rice (closest approved-source analogy), as HPB does not report saturated fat for this specific entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient panels, and moisture data for this specific Thai basil pork rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (72 × 66.4 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 47.8; a conservative mixed-dish estimate of 24.8 is reported to account for protein and fat co-ingestion slowing gastric emptying. The dish is not halal-certified as standard preparation uses oyster sauce which may contain shellfish. Chilli content varies by vendor; individuals with nightshade sensitivity or rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution.
